Services scalability and foundation models such as Amazon Bedrock. Despite these successes, in August 2024, Exscientia underwent 
a major change, being acquired by Recursion Pharmaceuticals in a
Fig. 3. Clinical-stage pipelines of leading AI-driven drug discovery companies. Five AI-focused biopharmaceutical companies, namely Exscientia, Insilico Medicine, Recursion
Pharmaceuticals, BenevolentAI, and Schr € 
odinger, are shown with selected pipeline compounds, including discovery code names, primary molecular targets and current clinical
trial phases. Targets span kinases (eg, CDK7, PKC-theta, TNIK, CDC7, and Wee1/Myt1), transcriptional regulators (RBM39), signaling proteins (Trk and MALT1), and metabolic 
enzymes (PDE10). Clinical trial stages range from phase I to phase IIa. M. Dharmasivam, B. Kaya, A. Akinware et al.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
Pharmacological Reviews 78 (2026) 100102
4
$688 million merger aimed at creating an “AI drug discovery 
superpower.” 158—160 The merger, completed in late 2024, com-
bined Exscientia’s strength in generative chemistry and design 
automation with Recursion’s extensive phenomics and biological 
data resources. 158 Postmerger, Exscientia’s capabilities are being 
integrated to enhance the combined platform, for example, using 
Exscientia’s AI to generate novel compounds that Recursion can 
validate in phenotypic assays. 71,76,161
This merger also rationalized the pipeline, with some of 
Exscientia’s earlier programs being paused or discontinued as the 
new entity aligned on its highest-value assets. 158 In summary, 
Exscientia’s journey illustrates both the promise of AI design 
(multiple “fast-to-clinic” candidates and productive partnerships) 
and the challenges, namely pipeline pruning and a need to bolster 
biological validation, which the Recursion collaboration and deal 
seek to address. 2. Clinical impact
To date, none of Exscientia’s AI-designed drugs have reached 
phase III trials, but several are in, or entering phase I/II. 42,140,162 The 
true test will be whether these compounds show superior clinical 
outcomes or success rates versus conventionally discovered drugs. Nonetheless, the company’s reported metrics are impressive.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
For 
example, 1 program examining a CDK7 inhibitor achieved a clinical 
candidate after synthesizing only 136 compounds, whereas 
traditional programs often require thousands. 49,163,164 This sug-
gests that AI—driven efficiency gains can be achieved streamlining 
medicinal chemistry workflows. Going forward under Recursion’s umbrella, Exscientia’s gener-
ative AI will be paired with large-scale in-house wet-lab data, a 
combination anticipated to further improve success rates. 77,165 As 
Chief Executive Officer, Andrew Hopkins noted that the best way to 
create many novel medicines is to pair Exscientia’s design platform 
with strong partners. 44,148 This supports the concept that AI is
most powerful when augmented with domain expertise and 
robust experimental feedback loops. B. Recursion pharmaceuticals: High-throughput phenotypic 
discovery
Recursion Pharmaceuticals was founded in 2013 in Salt Lake 
City (USA) and has taken a distinct AI approach centered on phe-
nomics, the large-scale mapping of cellular phenotypes (visual 
readouts of cell biology) to discover drug opportunities in an un-
biased manner. 82 Instead of starting with a specific protein target, 
Recursion’s platform interrogates disease biology by systemati-
cally perturbing cell models and analyzing the results with com-
puter vision and ML.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
71,166,167 At the heart of this technology is the 
Recursion operating system, an integrated system of automated 
wet-lab infrastructure and AI algorithms. 168,169
In Recursion’s automated lab, one of the world’s largest in the 
biotech sector, millions of experiments are conducted weekly. For 
example, human cells, including many induced pluripotent stem 
cell—derived disease cell types, are treated with compounds or 
genetic knockouts, then imaged under high-content micro-
scopes. 2,71,170 Notably, high-content microscopy refers to a type of 
automated, computer-assisted imaging system that combines 
advanced microscopy with image analysis software to extract large 
amounts of quantitative biological data from cells or tissues. Deep convolutional neural networks convert each microscopy 
image into a rich phenotypic analysis, embedding essentially a 
numerical profile of the cellular state observed. 171 By comparing 
these profiles, Recursion’s AI can detect when a treatment rescues a 
disease-associated phenotype, even if the mechanism is un-
known. 172 This allows the platform to identify active molecules or 
genetic modifiers with therapeutic potential, yielding hits that a 
hypothesis-driven approach might overlook. It also generates
Fig. 4. Exscientia small-molecule programs and current status in 2025.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
Each row shows the compound, primary target, and development stage; CDK7 (ELUCIDATE, NCT05985655) 
is active, REC-4539 (LSD1) has IND clearance, MALT1 is IND-enabling, the A2A program is discontinued, and DSP-1181 is not active. M. Dharmasivam, B. Kaya, A. Akinware et al. Pharmacological Reviews 78 (2026) 100102
5
massive relational datasets (Recursion has over 21 petabytes of 
image data, mapping trillions of gene and compound relation-
ships). 173 Recursion then follows up on these AI-generated leads 
with traditional validation, for example, identifying the compound’s 
target via proteomics and testing efficacy in animal models. 1. Platform evolution and 2024—2025 updates
Recursion’s phenotype-first strategy has enabled both drug 
repurposing and novel drug discovery. 172 A notable example is 
REC-994, a previously “shelved” compound identified by Re-
cursion’s AI platform that reversed cellular defects in cerebral 
cavernous malformation, a rare neurovascular disease, and 
advanced to a phase II trial (Table 1). 71,74—76,168 Leveraging phe-
nomics, the company also mapped genetic modifiers that rescue 
disease phenotypes to pinpoint and dissect promising targets. 71,76
By 2023, Recursion had 5 molecules in clinical trials, spanning 
rare diseases and oncology (Table 1). 158 However, clinical successes 
were inconsistent.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
In the SYCAMORE phase II trial of REC-994 (SYC-
AMORE is the official name of the Phase II clinical trial NCT05085561 
evaluating REC-994 for cerebral cavernous malformation), the com-
pound met its primary safety endpoint but demonstrated only 
modest, nonsignificant trends toward reduced lesion volume on 
magnetic resonance imaging and improved modified Rankin scale 
scores at the highest dose, without clear patient- or physician-re-
ported benefit. Although these results provided proof-of-concept for 
safety and exploratory efficacy, the lack of definitive clinical benefit 
ultimately led Recursion to discontinue the program. 71,74,77,158
Long-term extension data ultimately were not encouraging, 
and Recursion decided to discontinue further development of REC-
994. 75,158,174 Similarly, another early program (REC-2282 for 
neurofibromatosis type 2; Table 1) was halted after phase II trials 
due to insufficient efficacy. 158,175,176 These setbacks prompted 
Recursion to restructure its pipeline in 2024—2025, focusing on 
areas with stronger preliminary data. Notably, in November 2024, Recursion acquired Exscientia, 
bringing in Exscientia’s pipeline of AI-designed oncology com-
pounds. 71,158,165 By mid-2025, the combined company identity 
narrowed to 6 active development programs, 4 in oncology and 2 
in rare diseases.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
72,77,158 Several earlier programs were depriori-
tized, either discontinued or slated for out-licensing, 158 as Recur-
sion doubled down on opportunities where its integrated “data + 
AI” approach had the highest potential impact. 72
Recursion has massively scaled up its AI and data capabilities. 32 
In July 2023, it received a $50 million strategic investment from 
NVIDIA (a leading high-performance computing and graphics pro-
cessing company widely used for AI model training) and began 
leveraging NVIDIA’s latest supercomputing hardware. 32,177,178 By 
2024, Recursion built BioHive-2, a dedicated AI supercomputer with 
2 exaflops of performance (504 NVIDIA H100 graphics processing 
units) to train its models. 76,179 This enabled the development of large 
“foundation models” on Recursion’s cell image data. For example,
the Phenom model suite was trained on >3.5 billion images, learning 
generalizable representations of cellular biology. 173,179
A version of this, known as Phenom-ß, was made available on 
NVIDIA’s cloud for external use. 179 In 2025, Recursion open-sourced 
Boltz-2, a billion-parameter generative model for predicting protein 
3D structure and ligand binding affinities, achieving near physics-
accuracy, but 1000× faster.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
180 Boltz-2 was developed with the 
Massachusetts Institute of Technology and NVIDIA and was down-
loaded by over 40,000 users within weeks, demonstrating Re-
cursion’s growing role as a cutting-edge AI research contributor. The company also integrated multiomics patient data via part-
nerships with Tempus, Helix, and others to connect its phenomics 
findings to real-world clinical populations. 180 These efforts reflect 
Recursion’s vision of a “Virtual Cell,” namely a computational 
platform that can predict how complex biological systems will 
respond to interventions. Enhanced by Exscientia’s chemistry AI, 
Recursion can now not only find hits phenotypically but also design 
new molecules to exploit those insights. 32,71 Already, the merged 
entity reported hitting a $7 million milestone from Sanofi in 2025, 
as that partnership advanced 4 programs to discovery milestones in 
just 18 months. 72,76,77,180 Partnerships with Roche/Genentech 
(neuroscience, oncology) and Bayer (undruggable targets) are also 
continuing and are yielding vast datasets, such as a trillion-cell 
clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats pheno-
typic map for neuroscience (Table 2).
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
180,181
The combined pipeline of Recursion (post-Exscientia merger) 
as of mid-2025 165,180 includes oncology (CDK7 inhibitor REC-617 in 
phase I/II; RBM39 degrader REC-1245 in phase I/II) and rare dis-
ease (eg, REC-4881 for familial adenomatous polyposis in phase II) 
and highlights key partnership programs with Roche, Sanofi, 
Bayer, and Merck KGaA (Table 2). The merger has created a 
diversified pipeline with multiple near-term readouts. 2. Reflections
Recursion’s approach demonstrates the power of coupling AI 
with massive proprietary data. It has arguably built one of the largest 
vertically integrated datasets in biotech, and these data “flywheel,” 
that is more experiments → better models → more discoveries, 
could yield increasing returns. However, the company’s early clinical 
results demonstrate that AI-discovered hits are not guaranteed 
“home runs,” human biological responses can surprise, and in vivo 
efficacy must be proven case by case. 71 The decision to merge with 
Exscientia underscores that combining strengths does provide a path 
forward. In this case, Recursion gained cutting-edge generative 
chemistry and several clinical-stage compounds, whereas Exscien-
tia’s programs benefited from Recursion’s biological expertise and 
infrastructure. 158,165 This convergence of AI capabilities with bio-
logical expertise is a broader trend across the field.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
From a success standpoint, Recursion has demonstrated clear 
achievements in productivity, ranging from conducting millions of 
experiments weekly to identifying novel targets and pathways,

6
forging strategic partnerships, and securing over $1 billion in po-
tential milestones through collaborations and merger deals. How-
ever, the key challenge lies in translating these advances into 
meaningful patient outcomes, particularly as the merger anticipates 
more than 10 clinical readouts by 2025—2026.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
158 An improved suc-
cess rate, or even a single breakthrough within the next 2 years, could 
be highly impactful in validating the phenomics-driven paradigm. Furthermore, as in other areas of the pharmaceutical sector, 
conceptualization, execution, and translation are highly capital-
intensive, requiring sustained financing to maintain progress. This is evident in Recursion’s journey, having spent hundreds of 
millions to build its platform, and only through stable partnerships 
and revenue generation from its legacy software arm (via 
Exscientia) does it project a cash “runway” into 2027. 180 The 
company’s strong financial position (over $500 million in cash as 
of mid-2025) 180 and backing from tech investors such as NVIDIA 
indicate confidence that this data-heavy approach could pay off. Nonetheless, Recursion exemplifies the “TechBio” model: a syn-
ergy of big data, high-performance computing, and biology, 
betting that smart algorithms can uncover therapies that humans 
alone might miss. 182,183
C. Insilico Medicine: End-to-end artificial intelligence and 
generative successes
Insilico Medicine (Hong Kong and New York-based, founded 
2014) exemplifies end-to-end AI-driven biotech, integrating AI at
every stage from target discovery to clinical trial design.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
184,185 Its 
comprehensive Pharma.AI platform comprises interconnected 
modules: PandaOmics for AI-powered target identification (min-
ing omics and literature for novel disease drivers), Chemistry42 for 
generative molecule design, and InClinico for clinical trial outcome 
prediction (Table 3). 6,168,186,187 Using this “toolkit,” Insilico seeks to 
both discover first-in-class targets and generate drug candidates 
against them, a “full-stack” AI approach. The company’s ethos is to significantly reduce time and cost in 
drug R&D by algorithmically exploring the biological and chemical 
space faster than traditional methods. 188,189 A signature achievement 
came in 2021—2022, when Insilico’s AI system identified a novel 
fibrotic disease target, the enzyme TNIK, for IPF. It then generated a 
small-molecule TNIK inhibitor (ISM001-055; now Rentosertib; Fig. 5) 
against that target. 2,8,11,190 This program advanced from an AI—driven 
hypothesis to a clinical candidate in about 18 months, 2,30 making it 
one of the first examples of an AI-discovered target and AI-designed 
molecule entering human trials. 188
1. Pipeline and 2024—2025 progress
Insilico’s lead drug, ISM001-055 (Rentosertib), has become a 
milestone for the field, the first fully AI-developed drug to reach 
phase II trials.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
11,191 In 2023, Insilico initiated parallel phase IIa trials 
of ISM001-055 in IPF patients in China and the United 
States. 28,190,192,193 The China phase IIa trial in 2024 consisted of a 
12-week, placebo-controlled readout that led to positive results 
(Table 3). In fact, the drug met its primary safety endpoint and

text:
Dharmasivam, B. Kaya, A. Akinware et al. Pharmacological Reviews 78 (2026) 100102
7
demonstrated a dose-dependent improvement in lung function at 
the highest dose, whereas placebo patients continued to 
decline. 194 Specifically, a 60 mg dose of Rentosertib daily led to 
a +98 mL mean increase in forced vital capacity versus a − 20 mL 
decline on placebo over 3 months (Table 3). 11,28
These latter results suggested an encouraging early efficacy 
signal in a progressive and fatal disease with no curative therapy, 
with the results being published in Nature Medicine in June 2025. 11 
Hence, targeting TNIK appears safe and may provide benefit, 
warranting larger and longer trials. 11 Insilico is now planning late-
stage trials for IPF (potentially phase IIb/III) to start by the fourth 
quarter of 2025 (Fig. 5). 28 Notably, in March 2025, the United 
States Adopted Names Council officially approved “Rentosertib” as 
the nonproprietary generic name for the drug previously known 
by its code, ISM001-055, underscoring its maturity as a drug 
candidate (Fig. 5). 195
Beyond IPF, Insilico has rapidly expanded its clinical pipeline.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
It 
has 2 other AI-designed compounds in clinical trials, 1 is ISM3312, 
a novel oral immunomodulatory agent for coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) and other viral infections, which completed 
phase I studies in 2024 with positive results (showing safety in 
healthy volunteers). 60,196—198 Another is an AI-designed USP1 
enzyme inhibitor for cancer (ISM3091), which received IND 
approval in 2023 196,199,200 and is entering phase I. Insilico has also 
generated preclinical candidates for oncology applications, for 
example, a pantranscriptional enhancer activator domain family of 
transcription factor (TEAD) inhibitor for solid tumors 196 and other 
diseases. Impressively, the company reported a 100% success rate in 
advancing AI-nominated compounds to IND, that is, none of its AI-
designed preclinical candidates have been terminated before 
reaching clinical trials. Although the sample size is small, these 
results suggest that the application of AI may be improving early-
stage success by filtering out unsuitable compounds. Insilico’s 
productivity is fueled by continual platform upgrades. For
example, it incorporated AlphaFold2 and quantum computing into 
its discovery workflows, 196,201 and, in 2023, it launched a genera-
tive biology tool (“ChatPandaGPT”) for automating hypothesis 
generation.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
187,196,202
The company also secured significant partnerships, most 
notably a $1.2 billion multitarget deal with Sanofi in 2022, 168,196 
under which Sanofi is leveraging Insilico’s platform to discover 
new targets and molecules for up to 6 diseases. Additionally, 
Insilico closed a $95 million Series D in 2022 and a $110 million 
Series E capital-raising financing round in 2023 to fund its pipeline 
expansion. 168 With these resources, Insilico now boasts 20+ pre-
clinical programs spanning fibrosis, oncology, immunology, etc, 
many aiming at first-in-class targets. 2. Outlook and significance
Insilico’s rapid progress into phase II is a proof-of-concept for 
the field that the application of AI can deliver a novel drug 
candidate with clinical activity. 11 The TNIK inhibitor for IPF is 
particularly noteworthy as it introduced a completely new target 
for a difficult-to-treat disease via AI. 194 If Rentosertib ultimately 
succeeds in late-stage trials, it could become the first AI-
discovered drug to market, potentially in the later years of this 
decade. 11 Even now, the early success of AI has energized the in-
dustry. In fact, 1 commentary noted that Insilico’s IPF program 
represents a “proof-of-concept success for AI—driven drug dis-
covery.” 194,203 That said, challenges remain.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
No late-stage outcomes are known yet, and it is possible that 
some AI-designed drugs may not succeed in advanced clinical 
trials (phase II/III), given the inherent complexity of human bio-
logical systems, where drug responses remain challenging to 
predict. Moreover, Insilico’s strategy of pursuing novel biology and 
new targets carries a high risk, as historically, first-in-class 
mechanisms have a lower success probability than validated 
targets. Fig. 5. The TNIK inhibitor, ISM001-055, from 2019 target prioritization, through a 79-compound campaign and fibrosis model validation, to multinational phase 0—II trials 
(2022—2025). EMT, epithelial to mesenchymal transition; FMT, fibroblast to mesenchymal transition. M. Dharmasivam, B. Kaya, A. Akinware et al. Pharmacological Reviews 78 (2026) 100102
8
The company will need to demonstrate that its AI platform can 
sufficiently derisk these novel targets to improve industry aver-
ages. On the positive side, early indicators of drug safety are 
promising. Preliminary data from AI-native biotech companies 
suggest phase I success rates of ~80%—90% (compared with 
traditional industry averages of ~40%—65%), 139,204,205 although the 
sample size remains small and long-term outcomes past phase II 
are not yet proven.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
2 Insilico’s comprehensive approach, which 
unifies target discovery, chemistry, and clinical design AI, also 
exemplifies a trend of platform convergence, where AI companies 
aim to own the whole value chain. With substantial financing, growing partnerships, and 
increasing international recognition, the company expanded to 
new R&D centers in Abu Dhabi in 2022—2023, 196 establishing 
Insilico as a key player. The company is leading the charge in AI in
drug discovery into its next phase, and delivering not just candi-
dates faster, but potentially effective drugs to patients. D. BenevolentAI: Knowledge graphs, repurposing, and a cautionary 
tale
Founded in 2013, London-based BenevolentAI has been a 
pioneer in applying AI to target identification and drug repur-
posing. 188,206,207 Its platform centers on a vast knowledge graph 
that integrates scientific literature, biomedical databases, omics 
data, and clinical information (Fig. 6). 208,209 By applying natural 
language processing and graph ML, BenevolentAI’s system can 
propose novel links between genes, diseases, and compounds that 
might not be obvious to human researchers. 208,210,211 The goal is to 
generate hypotheses for either new targets in disease or new uses 
for existing drugs.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
Once a target is selected, AI models then assist medicinal 
chemists in designing or identifying molecules, including scanning 
for existing drugs that hit the target, enabling fast repurposing. A 
flagship success of this approach was the 2020 identification of 
baricitinib, which is an already-approved Janus Kinase inhibitor 
for rheumatoid arthritis, as a potential treatment for COVID-19. In 
this case, Benevolent’s algorithms flagged the potential of bar-
icitinib from its knowledge graph in a matter of days. 212—214 Bar-
icitinib went on to show a significant mortality benefit in patients 
with COVID and received emergency use authorization, becoming 
one of the first AI-predicted therapies to reach patients 
(Fig. 6). 26,215 This demonstrated the power of AI—driven repur-
posing, especially in an urgent scenario. 1. Pipeline and 2024 updates
BenevolentAI also invested in an internal pipeline of de novo 
drug programs, aiming to validate novel targets from its AI plat-
form. 5,209,211,216 By 2022, it had advanced at least 2 novel small 
molecules into the clinic: BEN-2293 (Table 4), a topical pan-Trk 
inhibitor for atopic dermatitis (eczema), and BEN-8744, an oral 
PDE10 inhibitor for ulcerative colitis. 10,90,186 Unfortunately, these 
programs highlight the risks and realities of AI-hypothesized 
biology.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
39,87
In 2023, BEN-2293 became the first high-profile failure of an 
AI-derived drug in clinical trials. It failed to show efficacy in a 
phase IIa trial in eczema (91 patients). The drug, aimed at blocking 
Trk-mediated itch signaling in the skin, was safe and well-
tolerated, but it did not significantly improve eczema severity or 
itch compared with placebo. 89
This was a major setback. Although Benevolent’s AI—proposed 
target (pan-Trk) appeared biologically plausible, the trial outcome 
suggested that either the target was not central to eczema path-
ophysiology, or the topical drug failed to reach its site effectively, 
or lacked sufficient target selectivity. As a result, BenevolentAI 
shelved BEN-2293 after years of development, illustrating that 
even AI-identified targets must face the ultimate test of human 
biology, and some will not pan out. 186
On a more positive note, BEN-8744 (PDE10 inhibitor; Table 4) 
successfully completed phase I trials in healthy volunteers and, in
Fig. 6. BenevolentAI’s knowledge-graph approach and milestones. Founded in 2013, 
Baricitinib repurposed for COVID-19 (2020), Initial Public Offering (IPO) via Special 
Purpose Acquisition Company (SPAC) (2022), BEN-2293 phase IIa outcome and Merck 
deal (2023), TechBio pivot (2024), and extended runway (2025), supported by 
AstraZeneca and Merck partnerships.

9
2023, entered a phase Ib trial in patients with ulcerative coli-
tis. 16,51,197,217 PDE10 was a novel target for inflammatory bowel 
disease suggested by Benevolent’s platform, and if this agent shows 
efficacy in upcoming phase II trials, it would vindicate the approach. BenevolentAI also has preclinical programs, such as an amyotrophic 
lateral sclerosis (ALS) project implementing a RAR-α/γ agonist that 
is in partnership discussions. 218 However, due to the mixed out-
comes and a challenging funding climate, 2023—2024 was a tur-
bulent period for BenevolentAI.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
After going public via a Special Purpose Acquisition Company in 
late 2021/early 2022 at a postmoney valuation of €1.5 billion 
(~$1.7 billion), the company’s lead drug failure and constrained 
capital flow prompted a strategic restructuring. In April 2021, 
BenevolentAI laid off ~30% of its workforce, closed its US office, and 
scaled back its software product ambitions (namely, the Knowl-
edge Exploration Tools), as part of a shift back to its “TechBio” roots 
(Fig. 6). 219,220 The company de-emphasized venturing into late-
stage drug development alone and instead prioritized offering its 
AI capabilities in partnership with others earlier in development to 
mitigate balance-sheet risk and extend its cash runway into late in 
the third quarter of 2025. 219
Benevolent also put any plans for standalone software products 
on hold (it had considered selling its platform as a software-as-a-
service offering, but opted not to). 221 By late 2024, the company 
even contemplated delisting from the Amsterdam exchange and 
going private as it restructured and extended its cash runway into 
2025 by cutting costs.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
219
Despite the setbacks, BenevolentAI still holds significant part-
nerships, notably a long-running collaboration with AstraZeneca 
(since 2019) on multiple targets, which was extended through 
2025 after yielding some promising projects. 222 In September 
2023, Merck KGaA also signed a 3-target deal with BenevolentAI 
(in parallel with 1 with Exscientia) with up to $594 million in 
milestones. 219 These partnerships provide external validation and 
potential upside if partnered programs succeed. The UK government has additionally supported BenevolentAI’s 
work (eg, funding for an AI Mission to explore drugs for dementia). However, overall, BenevolentAI’s recent chapter serves as a 
cautionary tale. Despite cutting-edge AI and strong science, the 
company faced the harsh reality of drug development. That is, 1 
major trial failure can force a pivot, especially for mid-sized 
biotech companies without other revenue. 2. Analysis 
BenevolentAI’s contributions should not be overlooked. 85 It 
demonstrated how AI—driven knowledge mining can yield real 
results; the baricitinib success likely saved many lives in the 
pandemic. 212,213,223 It also advanced new pharmacology, for 
example, PDE10 in colitis, etc, that might still yield benefits. 16,51 
The company’s struggles underscore key challenges for AI drug 
hunters, with the quality of input data being a critical factor.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
It is 
well known that AI predictions are only as good as the evidence 
base; significant gaps or biases in the biomedical literature can 
mislead AI models. 219 Even a correctly identified target may 
require optimal pharmacological intervention. For instance, systemic Trk inhibition could be required for the 
treatment of eczema, rather than simple topical application. Moreover, BenevolentAI’s story highlights the financial challenge. AI may accelerate discovery, but it does not remove the high costs 
of clinical trials. As the first generation of AI biotechs matures, we 
may see more mergers, partnerships, or refocusing on core com-
petencies (as in the case of Benevolent), rather than going it alone 
all the way to phase III trials. Encouragingly, BenevolentAI’s shift to a tech provider model 
could bear fruit if it secures new deals. With its rich knowledge
graph, often cited as one of the most comprehensive in biotech, 
and a multiyear head start in AI—driven target discovery, it re-
mains a key player, now contributing largely via partnerships.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
The 
international perspective is notable too; BenevolentAI was one of 
Europe’s AI biotech flagships, and its ups and downs will inform 
policymakers, for example, the UK investments in AI for life sci-
ences, about how best to support innovation while managing 
risk. 114
In summary, BenevolentAI proved the potential of AI in 
uncovering both new uses for old drugs and entirely new targets, 
but it also illustrated that AI is not magic. Rigorous validation and a 
solid business strategy are still paramount to turn AI insights into 
approved medicines. E. Schr € 
odinger: Physics-based simulation meets machine learning
Schr€ 
odinger, Inc. is headquartered in New York and was foun-
ded in 1990 (Table 5). It is a unique case among this cohort of 
companies, being an established leader in computational chem-
istry software that expanded into AI—driven drug discov-
ery. 100,190,224,225 Schr€ 
odinger’s core technology is a physics-based
molecular simulation platform, which for decades has been used 
in structure-based drug design, for example, virtual screening and 
free-energy calculations for binding affinity (Fig. 7). 226,227 Around
2018, Schr € 
odinger began pairing these physics-based methods,
like docking and molecular dynamics, with modern ML algorithms 
to enhance predictive power.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
228 The result is a “digital chemistry” 
platform that can design and optimize small molecules with high 
precision, provided a clear target structure is available. 10
In practice, Schr € 
odinger’s approach excels when the protein
target is well-characterized; their tools can then enumerate ana-
logs and predict potency, selectivity, and properties such as 
permeability or stability using a mix of quantum mechanics, ML,
and statistical modeling. 229 Importantly, Schr € 
odinger has a dual
business model; it sells its software to pharma and biotech com-
panies, which is a profitable segment that funds its research and 
also pursues an internal drug pipeline via collaborations and 
proprietary programs. 100 This strategy provides financial stability 
and a breadth of real-world data from many users’ projects to 
“feed” its AI models. 99
By the mid-2020s, Schr€ 
odinger had contributed to multiple 
clinical compounds, often via partnerships. A notable success was 
the discovery of NDI-034858, a TYK2 inhibitor, in collaboration
with Nimbus Therapeutics (Fig. 7). Designed using Schr €odinger’s 
platform, it showed best-in-class phase II results for psoriasis and 
was acquired by Takeda for US $4 billion in 2022.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

10
agent is now in phase III clinical trials and could become one of the 
first approved AI-influenced drugs. Schr € 
odinger’s internal pipeline as of 2025 includes 3 clinical-stage
oncology programs, including: (1) SGR-1505 (a mucosa-associated 
lymphoid tissue lymphoma translocation protein 1 [MALT1; Fig.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
7] 
inhibitor) for lymphomas; (2) SGR-2921 (a cell division cycle 
7-related protein kinase[CDC7] inhibitor) for acute myeloid leukemia 
(AML) and (3) SGR-3515 (WEE1 G 2 checkpoint kinase/membrane-
associated tyrosine- and threonine-specific cdc2-inhibitory kinase 
dual inhibitor) for solid tumors (Table 5). 93,94,97,233—235
All 3 agents entered phase I trials in 2022—2024 and are ex-
pected to report initial data by late 2025. 233 Early indications are 
promising, with SGR-1505 demonstrating potent target suppres-
sion in lymphoma, and SGR-2921 achieving pharmacologically 
relevant exposures in acute myeloid leukemia patients with a 
manageable safety profile. Formal data readouts are expected
later. 96,100,233 Schr € 
odinger is also advancing preclinical candidates,
such as the Son of Sevenless homolog 1 inhibitor, SGR-4174, to 
target RAS-driven cancers, which showed strong tumor inhibition 
in models. 98,233 In the fourth quarter of 2024, the company’s 
collaborative ethos continued with it expanding a drug discovery 
collaboration with Eli Lilly, adding a new target to an ongoing
project. 100,236,237
Furthermore, Schr € 
odinger has active collaborations with BMS,
Novartis, and others. 96,100 Collaboration revenue from upfronts 
and milestones is expected to grow to $45—$50 million in 2025. 236 
These outcomes suggest its partners are advancing its pharma-
ceutical programs successfully.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
As 1 example, Schr € 
odinger earned
$5.7 million from a Novartis collaboration in the first quarter of 
2025, likely representing payments for a drug development pro-
gram achieving a lead optimization milestone. 233
On the technology front, Schr € 
odinger has embraced AI to
augment its physics-driven engines. 238 The company developed a 
proprietary predictive modeling suite for absorption, distribution, 
metabolism, excretion, and toxicity, which aligns with the FDA’s 
initiative to reduce animal testing by using computational 
methods. 233,239 In 2025, it published a Nature Communications 
article on a novel AI—driven crystal structure prediction method
that addresses polymorph risk in formulation. 233,240 Schr €odinger’s 
use of deep learning for hit generation was evidenced by a late 
2023 achievement using generative models to design a series of 
CDC7 kinase inhibitors that progressed rapidly to the clinic. 96,233
Schr € 
odinger often cites metrics such as “less than 100 com-
pounds synthesized to reach a development candidate” in some 
programs, reflecting how computational refinement can reduce
laboratory work. Financially, Schr € 
odinger is one of the stronger
Fig. 7. Schr € 
odinger’s physics-based and AI-driven platform integrates molecular modeling with ML, enabling software licensing partnerships and a clinical-stage drug discovery
pipeline.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
M. Dharmasivam, B. Kaya, A. Akinware et al. Pharmacological Reviews 78 (2026) 100102
11
performers; for instance, in the first quarter of 2025, it reported 
$59.6 million revenue (+63% compared with the same period in 
the previous year), driven by a 46% increase in software sales (its 
licensing business). 233 Although still operating at a net loss due to 
heavy R&D investment, its operating expense outflow is modest 
relative to its peers, and it had over $500 million in cash, boosted 
by a 2021 Initial Public Offering to fund its pipeline. 1. Perspective
Schr € 
odinger stands out as a hybrid AI/pharmaceutical company
that has largely avoided the hype-induced pitfalls some startups 
face. 188,241 Its decades of domain experience in computational 
chemistry gave it credibility and a solid knowledge base to build 
upon with AI. This likely contributed to its higher success rate in 
generating viable clinical candidates. Many of its partnered pro-
grams have succeeded in development, and none of its internal 
programs have failed clinically yet, although they are early.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
However, Schr € 
odinger’s approach also has limitations; it is
most effective for drugging well-defined binding sites, and it is less 
applicable to novel target discovery or phenotypic exploration. 238 
In areas like those, companies like Recursion or BenevolentAI have
an edge. Thus, Schr € 
odinger has largely tackled targets that are
already genetically or biologically validated, such as MALT1 and 
WEE1, and optimized chemistry to achieve best-in-class 
molecules. 242
This is reflected in the fact that its biggest success (Nimbus 
TYK2) was against a known immunology target, but the AI/physics 
applications helped create a differentiated allosteric binder. 243 No
Schr € 
odinger-originated drug has been approved yet, but the re-
sults in the near future could change that if partners like Nimbus/
Takeda succeed in phase III trials. Schr € 
odinger’s business model,
featuring a profitable software arm and investments in drug dis-
covery, also offers a sustainability that pure-play biotech startups 
lack. It essentially allowed the company to be patient and scien-
tifically rigorous, without the same level of risk and pressure for 
immediate clinical success. The company frequently emphasizes 
maintaining “scientific leadership” and rigorous validation.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
For 
instance, it engages with regulators on advancing in silico 
methods, commenting on FDA initiatives for modeling in R&D. 233
In summary, Schr € 
odinger demonstrates how physics-driven AI
can accelerate lead optimization and improve molecule quality. Its 
success with partners attests that AI/ML can meaningfully 
augment traditional drug design workflows, delivering com-
pounds that are now in late-stage trials. The critical question for
Schr € 
odinger will be whether its internal pipeline yields a home-
run efficacy result, for example, if MALT1 or WEE1 inhibitors 
show clear patient benefit. 242 If so, it would validate not just the 
idea of AI speeding up drug discovery but also improving clinical 
success rates by crafting better drugs. F. Comparative summary
The 5 profiled companies illustrate diverse yet complementary 
approaches to how AI can be currently applied in drug discovery. Table 6 11,44,52,71—73,76,77,165,212,213,244,245 provides a side-by-side
comparison of their platforms, achievements to date, and 
challenges. II. Emerging players and global developments (2024—2025)
In addition to the 5 companies above, the past 2 years have seen 
new AI—driven drug discovery players rise to prominence with 
notable innovations or clinical progress. These include both 
startups and initiatives by tech giants.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
Below, we highlight a few
exemplary cases that underscore the international and techno-
logical expansion of AI in this field. A. Insitro (USA)
Founded by Daphne Koller in 2018, Insitro has pioneered the 
integration of human stem-cell disease models and ML. 172,246,247 
The company generates high-throughput functional genomic 
data, for example, clustered regularly interspaced short palin-
dromic repeats-edited induced pluripotent stem cell—derived cells 
modeling disease, and applies AI to identify targets predictive of 
patient outcomes. 168,248 Insitro’s approach bridges in vitro models 
and in silico analysis, yielding insights into diseases like nonal-
coholic steatohepatitis (NASH) and ALS. 249,250
Although Insitro has not yet announced its own clinically tar-
geted compounds, it has secured major pharma partnerships: with 
Gilead (since 2019, for NASH), 168 BMS (since 2020, a $25 million 
milestone in 2022 for discovering a novel ALS target), 168 and most 
recently Eli Lilly (2023) to find targets for metabolic diseases 
including metabolic-associated fatty liver disease. 168
Insitro also raised $400 million Series C financing in 2021 that 
reflects investor confidence.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
251 The company’s significance lies in 
its methodological innovation, as it is a leader in phenotypic AI 
(like Recursion), but uses human-induced pluripotent stem cell 
models and genetics to ensure relevance. If Insitro’s collaborations 
yield drug candidates, for example, Gilead has an Insitro-
discovered NASH target advancing in preclinical studies, 168,251 it 
will validate this data-driven target discovery approach. Insitro’s 
work underscores a broader trend of AI+lab integration and ex-
emplifies the West Coast US influence that integrates Silicon Valley 
AI with cutting-edge biology. It is anticipated that Insitro will 
announce its first clinical candidate in the near future, potentially 
marking another milestone for AI in drug discovery. B. Isomorphic Labs (UK)
Alphabet’s (Google’s) dedicated AI drug discovery subsidiary 
was launched in late 2021 and has quickly moved to the forefront 
by leveraging Google DeepMind’s AI breakthroughs. 114,252—254 
Isomorphic Labs builds upon AlphaFold, the revolutionary 
protein-folding AI, and other advanced AI techniques such as deep 
and reinforcement learning to model biological problems. 114,168 
In 2023, Isomorphic codeveloped AlphaFold2’s successor, 
namely AlphaFold3, and open-sourced it, providing even more 
accurate protein 3D predictions, which have been touted as a 
“game-changer” for structure-based drug design.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
168 Rather than 
focusing on a single pipeline, Isomorphic Labs positions itself as a 
platform to solve “the toughest challenges in biology and chem-
istry” with AI. In early 2024, it announced 2 landmark collabora-
tions (Eli Lilly and Novartis) that could be worth nearly $3 million 
in milestones. 168,255 These deals involve Isomorphic’s AI working 
on de novo small-molecule discovery for targets selected by these 
pharmaceutical companies. In July 2025, Isomorphic expanded the Novartis alliance to 
include 3 additional programs. 168,255 Such hefty deals signal big 
pharma’s strong interest in Big Tech’s AI prowess. Isomorphic Labs 
is also notable for its global talent, including Demis Hassabis, the 
cofounder of DeepMind, who leads the strategic vision, as well as 
top computational biologists and medicinal chemists recruited 
from industry. 253 Although Isomorphic has not disclosed specific 
drug compounds as yet, there are reports that it aims to have at 
least 1 AI-designed molecule in clinical trials by late 2025. 2,256 If 
achieved, that would mark the entry of big technology companies 
directly into drug development. M. Dharmasivam, B. Kaya, A. Akinware et al.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
Pharmacological Reviews 78 (2026) 100102
12
Isomorphic’s emergence reflects an international dynamic, 
with the United Kingdom becoming home to this Google spinout, 
bolstering the local biotech ecosystem and aligning with the UK 
government’s ambitions to lead in AI for life sciences. It also un-
derscores how tech giants are converging with pharma, bringing 
vast computing resources and AI expertise, for example, Deep-
Mind’s protein models to accelerate drug R&D. C. Atomwise (USA)
One of the earliest AI drug startups, Atomwise was founded in 
2012 in San Francisco. Atomwise focuses on structure-based deep 
learning, 257—259 with its AtomNet platform being a pioneer in 
applying convolutional neural networks to predict small molecule 
binding to protein targets, essentially performing virtual screening
in silico. 168,260,261
Atomwise built a library of over 3 trillion synthesizable com-
pounds that its AI can search for hits. 262 After years of providing 
services and partnerships, Atomwise achieved a milestone in late 
2023 by nominating its first internal development candidate, an 
AI-discovered TYK2 inhibitor for autoimmune disease. 168,263,264 
This orally bioavailable, allosteric TYK2 inhibitor demonstrated 
promise in preclinical models for systemic lupus erythematosus, 
psoriasis, and inflammatory bowel disease and is slated for clinical 
trials in 2024.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
Atomwise also signed sizable partnerships, such as a multitarget 
research collaboration with Sanofi in 2022 (for up to 5 targets). 48,168 
In April 2024, the company published results of a study screening 
318 targets with AtomNet, finding novel hits for 235 of them, 
showcasing AI’s breadth as an alternative to high-throughput wet 
screening. 168 Atomwise’s progress is significant, as it validates the 
longevity of AI drug startups after a decade of R&D. It is finally 
entering the clinic, potentially achieving success by being among 
the first wave of AI-originated small molecules to be tested in 
humans. Its work also highlights the continuing importance of 
structure-based methods in the AI “toolkit” and discovering how 
combining AI with the enormous virtual chemical space can yield 
drug candidates that might be missed by human-led discovery. D. XtalPi and Chinese artificial intelligence biotech companies 
(China)
A noteworthy development is the rapid rise of AI drug discov-
ery companies in China, supported by significant funding and state 
support. XtalPi, originally known for AI-assisted crystal form 
prediction, has evolved into a full-fledged AI drug discovery plat-
form blending quantum physics, cloud computing, and ro-
botics.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape

text:
265,266 It raised hundreds of millions of dollars, including 
investment from Tencent and SoftBank, and has struck deals with 
Western pharmaceutical companies. In 2023, XtalPi signed a $250 million collaboration with Eli 
Lilly, 267 and in 2024, announced a landmark codevelopment 
partnership with a US pharma, DoveTree. The latter partnership 
was worth up to $6—$10 billion for multiple AI-designed drug 
candidates. 267,268 Similarly, the Chinese pharma giant, China Shi-
jiazhuang Pharmaceutical Group (CSPC), struck a deal worth more 
than US$5 billion with AstraZeneca in mid-2025, granting Astra-
Zeneca access to CSPC’s AI-discovered oncology portfolio. 267 These 
latter developments represent some of the largest AI—pharma 
deals on record. The Chinese startup, Helixon, focused on generative AI for bi-
ologics, signed a $1.7 billion licensing deal with Sanofi in 2025 
through its US subsidiary, Earendil, to deliver AI-designed anti-
body therapeutics.
title: Leading AI-Driven Drug Discovery Platforms: 2025 Landscape
